barriers.name: Voluntary credential presented as if it were legal permission
barriers.mechanism: Protected designatory letters, member directories and practising rules sit beside an open-entry legal market, so buyers and entrants can mistake a body rule for a state monopoly.
barriers.statedRationale: Exams, experience, ethics, CPD, complaints and discipline create a recognisable quality framework where the state does not reserve ordinary advice.
burden: People may pay for a long route before learning that the desired activity is unreserved.
Unaffiliated competent practitioners can be treated as unlicensed even when the word is legally wrong.
whoBenefits: Buyers who correctly use the signal and complaints route.
Members and bodies whose designation differentiates them.
whoBearsIt: Entrants paying fees/time and consumers trying to distinguish membership from legal authorisation.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Display three separate lines on every role: required by law, required only for the designation/body, and commonly requested by employers/buyers.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Body directories, conduct rules and discipline can add real accountability, while this corpus links them without calling them state licences.

evidence.locator: Unprotected title and voluntary consumer checks
CTA accreditation with no reserved activities
Open market, unaffiliated share and regulatory rationale

barriers.name: Employer academic filters stricter than the profession's entry rule
barriers.mechanism: Employers ration paid seats with degree classifications, UCAS points, school grades, tests and interviews that the underlying qualification or law may not require.
barriers.statedRationale: High application volume, predicted exam success, client-facing standards and limited training capacity.
burden: A candidate eligible for ATT or Foundations can still be excluded from a funded role.
The filter becomes an experience barrier because most credentials require workplace evidence.
whoBenefits: Employers seeking a cheap, consistent screening system and candidates who match it.
whoBearsIt: Career changers, candidates with non-standard education and people unable to self-fund an alternative.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Search technician, small-firm and apprenticeship routes with different filters; apply directly to the body where possible and distinguish employer criteria from legal/qualification criteria.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Equality law constrains discrimination, and flexible/Foundations routes provide alternative academic entry even though they cannot force an employer to hire.

evidence.locator: One live vacancy's six seats and academic filters

barriers.name: Experience paradox and sponsor/confirming-person dependence
barriers.mechanism: Passing exams is insufficient: work must be relevant, documented and accepted by employers, reviewers, qualified supervisors, sponsors or confirmers.
barriers.statedRationale: Observed practice and character evidence reduce the risk that test knowledge is mistaken for professional judgment.
burden: Entrants need qualifying work to qualify and often need qualification progress to obtain that work.
A workplace power relationship becomes part of credential infrastructure.
whoBenefits: Clients and professions receiving an experience standard; employers with a retention lever.
whoBearsIt: Candidates in narrow roles, unsupportive workplaces, self-employment or precarious employment.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Choose paid work that explicitly names the body's experience outcomes and confirmer; keep evidence personally accessible and ask transfer/sign-off questions before joining.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: ACCA permits experience across employers and SQE permits QWE across up to four organisations, reducing but not removing supervisor dependence.

barriers.name: First-pass pricing hides resit and fee-bearing exemption economics
barriers.mechanism: Published totals often assume every assessment passes once, while several bodies charge exam-equivalent exemption fees and resits repeat large fees; late entry can be much higher.
barriers.statedRationale: Assessment and exemption processing must be funded and equivalent standards maintained.
burden: Candidates with weaker preparation or failed attempts pay materially above headline totals.
Prior learning can remove study but not the corresponding body charge.
whoBenefits: Bodies/providers funding assessment infrastructure and candidates whose prior learning saves time.
whoBearsIt: Resit candidates, late bookers and people self-funding exemptions.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Expose first-pass body cost, recurring cost, provider cost and resit scenario separately; use free official materials and employer funding before buying a full course.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Published fee schedules and pass-rate data permit comparison when presented with definitions and dates.

evidence.locator: SQE first attempt and resit performance

barriers.name: Authorised employer, approved provider and sole assessment-platform dependence
barriers.mechanism: Completion depends on an approved employer/provider or contracted assessment infrastructure, not only the learner's knowledge.
barriers.statedRationale: Authorisation standardises training quality, assessment security and work verification.
burden: Geography, provider price, seat availability and vendor outages can block a ready learner.
The candidate has limited bargaining power over sign-off or delivery failures.
whoBenefits: Bodies/regulators seeking consistent delivery and authorised providers receiving routed demand.
whoBearsIt: Learners without a local/funded provider and employers carrying authorisation administration.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Verify the provider/employer and transfer/outage rules before paying; prefer employer-funded access and preserve personal copies of training evidence.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Published authorised lists, complaints routes and flexible ACCA/SQE work patterns offer some alternatives, depending on route.

barriers.name: A qualification is achieved once; status is rented annually
barriers.mechanism: Annual subscriptions, certificates, licences, CPD, returns, PII, AML and run-off duties continue after exams.
barriers.statedRationale: Ongoing competence, consumer protection, supervision and body operations require continuing controls rather than a lifetime exam pass.
burden: Fixed costs weigh more heavily on small and part-time practices.
Leaving membership can remove designatory letters even though knowledge and historic passes remain.
whoBenefits: Clients receiving ongoing standards and bodies/regulators funding supervision.
whoBearsIt: Members, practices and ultimately clients through fees.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Model total cost over five years and join only the status needed for the intended market; keep statutory duties even if voluntary membership is dropped.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Reduced-income/retired categories and proportionate PII formulas exist in some routes; public fee schedules enable planning.

barriers.name: Public registers and member directories look alike but prove different things
barriers.mechanism: A statutory practising register, an AML supervised-business list, a fee-payer list, a voluntary member directory and a firm directory can all be presented as a search box.
barriers.statedRationale: Registers let buyers verify status and discipline without disclosing private application files.
burden: Users can overread listing as competence, endorsement or regulation of every service.
Omissions may reflect directory scope rather than lack of all qualification.
whoBenefits: Buyers who understand the scope and listed practitioners/firms receiving a discoverability signal.
whoBearsIt: Consumers interpreting status and unlisted people whose lawful status is different rather than absent.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Every register link should expose `registerScope`, `whatListingProves`, `whatItDoesNotProve`, `omissions` and last-check date.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Official disclaimers and separate certificate/status fields can prevent overclaiming when surfaced prominently.

evidence.locator: ASA not endorsement and no general public register identified

barriers.name: Qualification redesigns make a timeless pathway false
barriers.mechanism: Entry dates select different syllabuses, assessments, fees, transition rights and providers; marketing often describes one route without its effective period.
barriers.statedRationale: Bodies update competence models for law, technology, employer needs and assessment delivery.
burden: A learner can buy materials for the wrong version or lose assumed transition credit.
APIs silently mix current, closing and future routes.
whoBenefits: Future employers/clients if redesigned competence improves and providers selling updated material.
whoBearsIt: Students near transition boundaries, employers and maintainers of guidance.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Store route version, registration window, first/last sitting and transition source; never overwrite current facts with announced future rules.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Official transition timetables and grandfathering rules can preserve progress when checked early.

evidence.locator: Current route and announced CTA redesign

barriers.name: Bodies educate, sell membership, represent and discipline the same profession
barriers.mechanism: One institution can derive income from students/members, advocate on policy and exercise member regulation or delegated AML/statutory functions.
barriers.statedRationale: Profession-led expertise can set relevant standards, pool education and fund regulation from the regulated community.
burden: Stronger enforcement can impose cost on the same members who fund and elect parts of the body.
Users may not see which institutional arm made a statement.
whoBenefits: Members receiving education/representation and the public receiving specialist standard-setting.
whoBearsIt: Members funding multiple functions and the public relying on internal separation/oversight.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Label each claim by capacity: educator, membership association, AML supervisor, statutory regulator or policy participant; publish income mix and oversight without alleging capture from structure alone.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Royal charters, charity duties, lay participation, regulatory boards, independent discipline and public/state oversight mitigate—without erasing—the tension.

evidence.locator: CIOT governance, membership/student income and functions
ICAEW learning, membership, regulatory and commercial income
ACCA operating income/expenditure and governance reporting

barriers.name: State digital filing creates demand for private compatible software
barriers.mechanism: Making Tax Digital requires compatible digital records/submission while HMRC supplies APIs rather than a full taxpayer-facing product for every workflow.
barriers.statedRationale: Digital records and automated transfer are intended to reduce error, modernise service and improve revenue administration.
burden: Businesses buy or adopt private software and bear setup, subscription, migration and integration costs.
Historical API/user data and accountant workflows can raise switching costs.
whoBenefits: HMRC from standardised digital submissions, software vendors from recurring demand and some users from automation.
whoBearsIt: Mandated taxpayers, advisers and small firms with legacy processes.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Offer open exports and interoperable calculation APIs; show free/low-cost options and make data portability a product requirement.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: HMRC publishes compatible-product lists and says it does not recommend vendors; competition and export standards can limit lock-in.

evidence.locator: Programme cost, customer cost and 2023 software observations
Private applications connect to government APIs under terms

barriers.name: Large partnership structure concentrates residual profit at the equity tier
barriers.mechanism: Large client-funded networks employ salaried pyramids and distribute residual profit to a smaller equity-partner group inside legally separate member firms.
barriers.statedRationale: Partnership ownership aligns senior professionals with capital, quality risk, client relationships and long-term firm performance.
burden: Headline average partner profit can obscure the much broader employee pay distribution and long promotion ladder.
Global branding can obscure which local legal entity contracts and bears liability.
whoBenefits: Equity owners in profitable years and clients needing international scale/specialist depth.
whoBearsIt: Employees competing for promotion and users who must identify the actual contracting member firm.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Publish employee salary and owner-profit measures separately, name geography/legal entity and do not infer tax-market share from audit or total firm revenue.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Legal notices, LLP accounts, regulatory separation and company-reported perimeters let careful users identify ownership and limits.

evidence.locator: UK fee income and partner distribution
Combined perimeter, separate-firm wording and partner result

barriers.name: Real statutory barriers for reserved legal, insolvency and enforcement acts
barriers.mechanism: Law reserves defined acts or offices and makes authorisation/certification an element of lawful performance, unlike voluntary status for general advice.
barriers.statedRationale: Litigation, client property/estates, insolvency control and coercive seizure create high public/third-party risk requiring competence, fitness, insurance/security and oversight.
burden: Years of training, work access, checks, insurance/bond and recurring oversight.
An API must stop at the boundary even if it can technically generate a document or action.
whoBenefits: Clients, courts, creditors, debtors and the public through accountable office-holders.
whoBearsIt: Entrants, authorised firms and consumers through fees/time.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Partner with the authorised person and encode responsibility: TaxSorted calculates and prepares data; the authorised provider exercises judgment, approves/submits reserved instruments, holds office or takes control.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Public registers, appeals/courts, discipline, PII/bond and defined statutory scope constrain monopoly and provide remedies.

barriers.name: One input field can move an API from information into supervised accountancy service
barriers.mechanism: HMRC's ASP guidance includes automated recording, analysis, calculation/reporting and customer-specific liability decisions; universal information delivered identically is treated differently.
barriers.statedRationale: Automation can scale the same money-laundering and customer-risk exposure as human accountancy service, so delivery method should not create a loophole.
burden: A product team must classify capabilities at design time, perform CDD and supervise the service even without human advice calls.
The narrow B2B exception requires operational dependence on every customer's supervision.
whoBenefits: The AML system and clients who receive controlled services; compliant suppliers gain clearer legitimacy.
whoBearsIt: Personalised calculation providers and customers supplying identity/evidence.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Keep a universal endpoint genuinely universal; put client-specific calculation behind an `aml_supervised_or_exception_verified` capability and prefer direct HMRC supervision when customer-by-customer exception proof is harder.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: HMRC publishes scope and a specific B2B exception, and businesses can seek professional advice/clarification for edge cases.

evidence.locator: Automated and customer-specific scope plus exception

barriers.name: HMRC production access and product recognition are separate private-facing gates
barriers.mechanism: Sandbox access, production application, security/OAuth terms and tax-specific recognition/listing are separate decisions with no general public application-decision register.
barriers.statedRationale: HMRC protects taxpayer data and service reliability and tests that filing software implements each tax specification.
burden: Security, accessibility, fraud headers, testing and ongoing version maintenance cost more than the published zero application fee.
Recognition wording can be overread as whole-product approval.
whoBenefits: Taxpayers/HMRC from controlled connections and recognised suppliers from distribution/listing.
whoBearsIt: Software builders and ultimately customers through build/maintenance prices.
barriers.lawfulLowFriction: Start with calculation/export, then obtain production access and recognition one capability/tax at a time; publish the exact recognised function and last test date.
barriers.countervailingSafeguard: Published terms, specifications, sandbox and supplier lists make much of the functional gate inspectable even though application decisions are not public.

evidence.locator: Sandbox, production, security and revocation terms

transparencyGaps.title: No comparable whole-route cost across qualifications
transparencyGaps.detail: Bodies publish different mixtures of registration, annual, exam, exemption and membership fees. Private tuition, VAT, resits, travel, employer funding, practice permission, PII and opportunity cost are not reported in one comparable format.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A first-pass body-fee total can look cheaper while the actual multi-year path is more expensive; this API exposes components and refuses false precision.

evidence.locator: ACA component schedule and route transition
Centre-set, annual, standard/late and exemption fees

transparencyGaps.title: Pay ranges do not establish a qualification premium
transparencyGaps.detail: Career profiles cover broad roles, company reports cover owners and service revenue, and body surveys use different populations/metrics. None isolates what the credential caused after experience, location, employer and selection are controlled.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Users must not read £25k–£55k adviser pay or seven-figure average partner profit as the expected return on ATT/CTA/ACA/ACCA.

transparencyGaps.title: No general public register of mandatory HMRC adviser-registration decisions identified
transparencyGaps.detail: HMRC provides ASA/registration processes and says registration is not endorsement, but this review found no public lookup equivalent to the Solicitors Register or insolvency-practitioner search for all registered advisers.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Clients cannot independently verify the new access status through one public list; professional-body and AML registers prove different things.

evidence.locator: Registration process with no public lookup linked

transparencyGaps.title: HMRC production API application decisions are not publicly auditable per applicant
transparencyGaps.detail: Terms and tax-specific supplier lists are public, but this review found no application-level register of production approvals, refusals, reasons, review times, security conditions or revocations across all developer apps.
transparencyGaps.consequence: The gate's operation and equality of treatment cannot be measured from public data; recognition lists show only some final product outcomes.

evidence.locator: Terms expose process, not a decision register

transparencyGaps.title: ACA, AAT, CTA and ACCA route transitions are moving targets
transparencyGaps.detail: The current ACA applies to new starters after July 2025, AAT Level 4 changes on 1 September 2026, CTA redesign begins for enrolments in autumn 2027/exams May 2028, and ACCA redesign starts September 2027.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Without effective dates, the same endpoint can incorrectly combine exams, fees and transition rights from incompatible versions.

transparencyGaps.title: No comparable current UK tax-advice or tax-software market-share dataset found
transparencyGaps.detail: Big Four revenue reports use different perimeters and service lines; vendor customer counts are not comparable UK tax-product shares. Audit concentration cannot be reused as tax-advice concentration.
transparencyGaps.consequence: This corpus can expose scale and business models but cannot responsibly rank market power or claim a tax oligopoly from the available measures.

evidence.locator: Dated package counts and free-option limitations

transparencyGaps.title: The UK insolvency authorisation route is not one uniform regime
transparencyGaps.detail: Great Britain currently has three RPBs under Insolvency Service oversight, while Northern Ireland has a separate legislative/oversight route and a fourth UK authorising body in the professions register.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A UK-wide record can overstate common rules, fees, authorisers or supervision unless jurisdiction is explicit.

evidence.locator: UK profession entry including Northern Ireland route
Three Great Britain RPBs and current oversight

transparencyGaps.title: Affiliation and complaints data do not isolate adviser quality
transparencyGaps.detail: Government consultation material describes market size, affiliation and risks, but public data do not provide a complete adviser-level outcome, error, complaint, redress and price dataset across affiliated and unaffiliated practices.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Neither affiliation nor open entry alone proves quality or harm; regulation options must not be sold as evidence already established.

evidence.locator: Body consumer guidance without whole-market outcome dataset

transparencyGaps.title: AML classification still depends on the facts of an API service
transparencyGaps.detail: HMRC guidance clearly includes automated client-specific calculation and excludes some universal information, but mixed products, subcontracting and the all-supervised-customer exception turn on contracts and operations, not an endpoint label.
transparencyGaps.consequence: The API can expose a conservative capability flag but cannot issue a binding perimeter ruling for an unseen business model.

evidence.locator: Scope examples and conditional B2B exception
Registration depends on actual business and supervisor

transparencyGaps.title: No comparable provider price, completion and pass-rate dataset
transparencyGaps.detail: Exam bodies list, approve or contract providers to different degrees, but no public dataset compares total price, candidate mix, completion, pass rates, complaints and refunds across ATT, CTA, ACA, ACCA, AAT and SQE preparation.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Listed/approved status cannot be translated into a value-for-money ranking, and students face marketing claims with limited common evidence.

evidence.locator: Assessment outcomes do not compare preparation providers comprehensively
